title: Financial Sophistication and Consumer Spending
abstract: Using detailed account-level data, this paper explores how financial sophistication affects consumers' spending responses to changes in income. I document that, controlling for liquidity, financially unsophisticated consumers display significant spending responses to predictable decreases in their disposable income. Furthermore, they have lower savings rates, fewer liquid savings, and higher debt-to-income ratios, leaving them more exposed to income shocks. Robustness tests, supported by anecdotal survey evidence, indicate that these results are driven by some consumers' lack of financial sophistication and their consequent failure to understand their financial contracts, rather than by random idiosyncratic shocks, rational liquidity management, or optimal inattention.

article.introduction:
What determines consumers' spending responsesto changes in income? Economists have long documented significant variation in consumers' spending responses, and the financial crisis of 2007 to 2008 highlights the role of spending responses in exacerbating financial shocks during a recession. To accurately model the economy and design effective fiscal and monetary policy, it is critical that we understand the underlying economic mechanisms.
Does financial sophistication play a role? Most traditional models assume that all consumers are financially sophisticated; for example, both the textbook buffer-stock models and current workhorse models with heterogeneous agents and multiple assets assume that all consumers are forward-looking with rational expectations. According to these models, any variation in savings and spending responses is due to idiosyncratic shocks and rational liquidity management rather than differences in financial sophistication. However, a growing literature shows that, in fact, a lack of financial sophistication affects many consumers' ability to make financial choices (Lusardi and Mitchell (2014), Agarwal, Chomsisengphet, and Lim (2017b)). This factor may complement and reinforce rational liquidity constraints, and—according to recent theoretical work—may drastically decrease the effectiveness of fiscal and monetary policy (Farhi and Werning (2019), Gabaix (2020)).
This paper provides empirical evidence on the effect of financial sophistication on consumers' spending responses, using account-level data for millions of U.S. consumers. The main result is that financial sophistication can affect spending responses through two channels. First, there is a direct effect: using a difference-in-differences (DID) event study of predictable changes in mortgage payments, I find that financially unsophisticated consumers display significantly greater spending responses to decreases in income, even when they have high levels of liquidity. Second, there is an indirect effect: financially unsophisticated consumers have lower savings rates, lower liquid savings, and higher debt-to-income ratios, which leaves them more exposed to unpredictable income shocks. Importantly, these two effects reinforce each other, exacerbating the impact of a lack of financial sophistication on spending responses.
To measure financial sophistication, I collaborated with a large U.S. bank to construct a deidentified panel data set containing transactions and balances from checking accounts, credit cards, and mortgage loans. The data sample is drawn from a universe of more than 30 million customers who held an account with the bank over the period of 2012 to 2017. Three strengths of the data allow me to jointly study financial decisions and spending: (i) daily frequency, (ii) linked credit cards, checking accounts, and mortgage balances, and (iii) detailed expenditure categories. Financial decisions appearing in the data include bill payments and refinancing, investment, and savings decisions. Spending comprises debit and credit card spending, cash withdrawals, and bill payments.
In the first part of the paper (SectionII), I construct a measure of financial sophistication based on financial mistakes. In the language of Choi et al. (2014), I proxy for decision-makingabilityusing a measure of decision-makingquality. Specifically, the financial sophistication measure is based on two simple financial mistakes related to credit and debit card transactions: incurring avoidable late fees and incurring avoidable overdraft fees. Following Stango and Zinman (2009) and Scholnick, Massoud, and Saunders (2013), a late fee or an overdraft fee is consideredavoidable, or afinancial mistake, if on the payment date the consumer has sufficient liquidity to cover both the required payment and a full month of consumption expenditures. This restriction is conservative, in that it provides a buffer for consumers to address future liquidity needs, and it limits the scope of the study to relatively wealthy consumers (those who carry at least enough liquidity to cover an average month of consumption expenditures). In view of this bias, one can expect the patterns documented in this paper to be more pronounced in other parts of the income and credit distribution, as financial mistakes are more prevalent among poorer individuals.1
The central premise of the analysis in this paper is that avoidable card fees are an indication of financial sophistication: consumers who incur many avoidable card fees are less likely to fully understand their financial contracts. That is, variation in decision-makingqualitycaptures variation in decision-makingability. I examine and discuss the validity of this premise from three different perspectives. First, I document that incurring avoidable card fees is a persistent behavior over a multiyear period. For example, a consumer who incurred more than one avoidable late fee last year is more than four times as likely to incur one this year, even after controlling for age, income, credit score, liquid assets, and total assets. Second, I show that incurring avoidable card fees is correlated with other financial mistakes studied in the literature, namely, lack of account optimization (Ponce, Seira, and Zamarripa (2017), Gathergood et al. (2019)) and lack of mortgage refinancing (Agarwal, Rosen, and Yao (2016), Keys, Pope, and Pope (2016)). Third, I consider potential alternative mechanisms driving late and overdraft fees. I find that consumers are invariant to the economic costs and to the transaction costs of the fees. These tests, together with the anecdotal evidence, support the view that financial mistakes are driven by individual characteristics related to consumers' financial decision-making abilities.
Using financial mistakes as a measure of financial sophistication, in the main analysis (SectionIII) I ask whether financial sophistication affects consumers' spending responses to a change in mortgage payments. The underlying assumption is that if unsophisticated consumers do not fully understand their financial contracts, they may display excessive sensitivity to changes in disposable income. For example, a consumer may be surprised by changes in her mortgage payments, and drastically cut spending in response, even when those changes would be perfectly predictable to the econometrician, who understands the mortgage contract.
The key challenge for identification here is that financially unsophisticated consumers who fail to fully comprehend their financial contracts are presumably also the ones with low liquid savings. It is therefore difficult to infer whether a spending response is the result of low liquid savings or a lack of financial sophistication. I address this challenge using a DID event study of consumers with a Home Equity Line of Credit (HELOC), a consumer loan collateralized by home equity.
The HELOC has two specific features that make it a useful setting for identifying the effect of a lack of sophistication. First, HELOC customers have higher incomes and higher levels of liquid assets than the sample-population average. For example, even among the most financially unsophisticated HELOC customers, the average annual income is almost $100,000 and the average level of liquid assets is more than $14,000. Second, and more importantly, the HELOC contract involves a predetermined decrease in disposable income, which can be used to distinguish the effect of a lack of sophistication from the effect of low liquidity.2In particular, HELOC payments are structured across two periods: adrawperiod (often 5 or 10 years), during which the HELOC is an open-ended and nonamortizing line of credit, and arepaymentperiod (often 10 or 20 years), during which the consumer pays both interest and principal. The reset from the draw period to the repayment period results in a discontinuous change in monthly payments of more than $450 on average.
The empirical specification is a DID test comparing the spending of financially sophisticated individuals before and after HELOC reset to the spending of financially unsophisticated individuals. I find that in the six months following HELOC reset, individuals cut their spending by $62 per month on average. When I examine financially sophisticated and financially unsophisticated individuals separately, I find that financially sophisticated individuals do not cut spending at all (the point estimate is −$18, which is statistically insignificant). By contrast, financially unsophisticated individuals cut their consumption by $162 per month more than financially sophisticated individuals, even controlling for age, income, total assets, liquid assets, and credit scores.
These results suggest that financially unsophisticated consumers do not fully understand the information presented to them in their HELOC contracts. Anecdotal evidence from surveys and newspaper articles supports this view. For example, in a recent survey, TD Bank found that more than one-quarter of homeowners with HELOCs did not know when their HELOC draw period would end. Only 19% knew that the monthly payments would increase when the draw period ended, and, surprisingly, 38% of those surveyed thought their payments would decrease (TD Bank (2016)).
An alternative explanation is that the spending response following HELOC reset is part of a financial plan. For example, consumers may time their spending on durable goods based on their mortgage payments. This hypothesis is not, however, borne out in the data. It turns out that following HELOC reset, financially unsophisticated individuals cut their spending on nondurables, such as fuel and groceries, as well as necessities, such as drugstore purchases and medical copayments.
In the second part of the empirical analysis (SectionIV), I ask whether financially unsophisticated consumers save less and have less financial “slack” in their budgets. A key result in the previous literature is that consumers who are closer to their borrowing constraints (those with low liquid assets, a high debt-to-income ratio, or a low credit score) display high spending responses (Johnson, Parker, and Souleles (2006), Jappelli and Pistaferri (2014), Baker (2018)). Does lack of financial sophistication, through this indirect channel, leave consumers more exposed to unpredictable income shocks?
I find that the answer is yes: savings rates are highly correlated with financial sophistication. The average savings rate of the most financially unsophisticated individuals is 7 percentage points lower than that of the most sophisticated individuals, even after controlling for age, income, credit score, and assets. This leaves financially unsophisticated consumers more exposed to income shocks. I find that financially unsophisticated consumers have significantly lower levels of liquid assets, higher debt-to-income ratios, and slightly lower credit scores. Furthermore, I find that financial sophistication not only correlates with contemporaneous measures of financial slack but also predicts future measures. For example, the consumers who made the most financial mistakes during 2013 and 2014 have 50% lower liquid assets and 10% higher debt-to-income ratios during 2015 and 2016 than consumers who did not make any financial mistakes during 2013 and 2014, even after controlling for age, income, and credit score.
To shed light on the mechanism driving lower savings rates, I examine the expenditures of financially unsophisticated individuals, broken down by type of expenditure. I classify expenditures asnecessitiesorluxuries, based on the shapes of their individual Engel curves. The data show that compared to financially sophisticated individuals, financially unsophisticated individuals spend a significantly higher share of their total expenditures on luxury goods, such as entertainment, electronics, jewelry and arts, and travel. One potential explanation for this result is that financially unsophisticated consumers also have a high propensity for indulgence (Banerjee and Mullainathan (2010), Vissing-Jørgensen (2021)).
In summary, the empirical results are consistent with the view that high spending responses occur when unsophisticated consumers do not fully understand their financial contracts, while they are inconsistent with such responses being mainly due to random idiosyncratic shocks, rational liquidity management, or optimal inattention. Moreover, the two main empirical results reinforce each other: lower savings rates and lower levels of liquid assets both exacerbate the spending responses caused by not fully understanding financial contracts. These findings point to the need for new models of consumers' savings and spending behaviors.
The main contribution of this paper is to shed light on an economic mechanism that drives consumers' spending responses to changes in disposable income.3A key result of the previous literature is that consumers with low levels of liquid savings have high spending responses, and consequently a growing theoretical literature uses liquid savings as a “sufficient statistic” for consumers' spending responses.4However, recent papers document significant spending responses even among consumers with high liquidity and even in reaction to predictable decreases in income (Kueng (2018), Olafsson and Pagel (2018a), Ganong and Noel (2019)). The findings in this paper suggest a unifying explanation via the notion of financial sophistication: financially unsophisticated consumers are more prone to low liquidity than financially sophisticated consumers, which makes them more likely to display high spending responses to unpredictable decreases in income. Indeed, unlike financially sophisticated consumers, financially unsophisticated consumers display high spending responses to decreases in income (even predictable decreases), even when they have high liquidity.
In addition, this paper's use of financial mistakes to measure financial sophistication contributes to an active literature that uses revealed choices to study differences in decision-making ability (Choi et al. (2014), Bhargava, Loewenstein, and Sydnor (2017)), and in particular to recent papers that relate financial mistakes to differences in individual behavior (Weber (2018), Cespedes (2019), Birru et al. (2023)). For example, in subsequent work, Weber (2018) replicates the measure of financial sophistication from this paper and finds that financially sophisticated individuals are more successful at starting and managing businesses. More broadly, this paper contributes to a larger literature on financial literacy (Bernheim (1995,1998), Agarwal et al. (2009a), Lusardi and Mitchell (2011), Lusardi and Mitchell (2014), Campbell (2016), Fong et al. (2021)).5
Finally, this paper may provide empirical underpinnings to a nascent literature that incorporates estimates from household and behavioral finance into models of consumers' savings and spending behavior. This literature includes the work of Pagel (2017), who shows that loss aversion can lead to excess consumption sensitivity in a life-cycle model, Lian (2023), who develops a theoretical model of consumers who misperceive their wealth and display excessive consumption responses to changes in income, and Kuchler and Pagel (2020), who use transaction-level data to measure consumers' awareness of their own present bias and to show that impatient consumers display higher consumption sensitivity. Other examples are the studies of Farhi and Werning (2019) and Gabaix (2020), who incorporate bounded rationality into New Keynesian models via level-kthinking and a “cognitive discounting” parameter, respectively. They show that bounded rationality may complement and reinforce rational liquidity constraints and, in certain cases, may drastically decrease the effectiveness of fiscal and monetary policy.
The rest of the paper proceeds as follows. I describe the data sample in SectionI. In SectionII, I construct the measure of financial sophistication, examine the validity of using avoidable fees as a measure of financial sophistication, and analyze alternative economic mechanisms. In SectionIII, I present estimates of the effect of financial sophistication on spending responses, and in SectionIV, I study the effect of financial sophistication on savings. In SectionV, I discuss potential implications of the results. SectionVIconcludes the paper.
article.I. Data:
In collaboration with a large U.S. financial institution, I assembled a data set that allows me to study financial choices, savings, and expenditure jointly. The data come solely from this institution, which I refer to as “my bank,” and the data set is stripped of any personal identifiers. Crucially for this paper, the data include both transactions and balances from checking accounts, credit and debit cards, and mortgage loans. In this section, I define the data sample, present the key variables used in the analysis, and discuss trends and seasonality.
The data sample is drawn from a universe of more than 30 million customers who held an account with my bank. The unit of observation is the customer-month, from November 2012 through June 2017.
In the primary analysis, I impose two restrictions on the sample. First, to focus on customers whose primary bank account is with my bank, I follow Ganong and Noel (2019) and limit the sample to customers with at least five monthly checking account outflows, where an outflow is defined as any debit, including a cash withdrawal, an electronic automated clearing house (ACH) payment, a paper check, or a debit card transaction. This criterion is conservative in that it probably drops some households who do bank primarily with my bank. Second, since I wish to observe card expenditure data, I limit the sample to customers who have at least one active credit or debit card with the bank. Here, I define an active credit or debit card as one that has had at least five transactions in a month at some point during the sample period.
Under these two restrictions, I draw a random sample of one million consumers who each have an active checking account and an active credit or debit card. Furthermore, from the first sample I draw a second, smaller sample consisting of 320,000 consumers who hold a HELOC with my bank. This second sample is used for the analysis of spending responses.
In the benchmark empirical analysis, I study the savings and expenditure behavior of individual consumers. That is, the unit of observation is a customer-month. In some cases, however, my bank observes spending for accounts belonging to other family members (e.g., spouses), which allows me to aggregate the analysis to the household level. (For example, most people link their bank accounts administratively when they get married; see TD Bank (2014).) The key empirical results concerning financially unsophisticated consumers—namely, that they save less and display larger spending responses—hold at this level of aggregation as well.
The main variables of interest are spending, income, assets, and liabilities.
Spending: The measure of spending that I construct comprises three components that capture just over half of all checking account outflows. These components are debit and credit card spending, cash withdrawals, and bill payments. (Credit card expenditures are counted in the month in which they occur, not the month in which the credit card bill is paid.) The remaining outflows comprise consumer debt payments, transfers to external accounts, and uncategorized outflows.6
I classify expenditures using two different systems, following the standard expenditure taxonomy for survey data (Lusardi (1996)). The categories in the first system aredurable,strict nondurable, andother nondurable; those of the second system areluxury,necessity, andother. For both classifications, I use the four-digit Merchant Category Code (MCC) that merchants report to the Internal Revenue Service. The unit of observation for all spending variables is the consumer-day.
Income: I construct income from two components that jointly make up 60% of inflows: (i) payroll paid using direct deposits and (ii) government income. The remaining inflow categories are other income, transfers from savings and investment accounts, and uncategorized inflows. The majority of uncategorized inflows are in the form of paper checks.
Assets: I use three measures of assets. The first is my bank's estimate of total assets, which is based on an internal statistical model that uses a combination of checking account activity, mortgage accounts, investment accounts, and third-party data sources. The other two measures are estimates of liquid assets. The first is the sum of balances across all checking and savings accounts within my bank. I refer to this measure aschecking account balances. The second measure is based on the bank's internal statistical model, which includes activity across checking, savings, and investment accounts, as well as third-party data. I refer to this measure asliquid assets. The main empirical results are valid for either measure.
Liabilities: As with assets, I use both internal bank estimates and direct measures to capture liabilities. My bank has an internal statistical model that predicts “total debt” based on activity across credit cards, mortgages, savings, and investment accounts, as well as third-party data. My direct measures of liabilities include revolving balances on credit cards, outstanding balances on mortgages, outstanding balances on HELOCs, and other consumer loans.
During the sample period, there was an upward trend in card spending and transactions (Federal Reserve Board (2017)). This trend reflects both higher expenditures and a shift in payment methods, as consumers made fewer purchases in cash and more using cards. (For example, Relihan (2017) finds that the average number of trips and average spending at grocery stores both trend upward over the sample period.) To control for this trend, I include year-quarter×$\times$ZIP code fixed effects; the reported results are identified from the remaining variation.
article.II. Measuring Financial Sophistication:
In this section, I construct a measure of financial sophistication based on financial mistakes. I describe the two financial outcomes that I classify as mistakes—avoidable late fees and avoidable overdraft fees—and I document how consumers who incur these fees differ across demographic and financial characteristics.
How pervasive are avoidable late fees? Panel A of Figure1shows the annual frequency of incurring an avoidable late fee. We see that more than half of the consumers in the sample have incurred an avoidable late fee at least once. Specifically, over the sample period, 47% of consumers have never incurred an avoidable late fee, 30% have incurred on average less than one per year, 11% have incurred on average between one and three per year, 9% have incurred on average between three and six per year, and 3% have incurred on average more than six per year.
How costly are avoidable late fees? The cost of incurring an avoidable late fee includes both the fee itself and an additional penalty term, as incurring a late fee is classified as credit card delinquency. Conditional on delinquency, the interest paid on outstanding balances increases to a penalty annual percentage rate (APR), which is often significantly higher than the usual APR.9For example, if a consumer who holds an average credit card balance of $1,000 forgets to pay the minimum payment of $25, then the cost of this financial mistake is $35 in a one-time late fee plus $12.49 in extra interest charges every month for the next six months. In this example, the cost of an avoidable late fee is $109.95.10The costs of avoidable late fees can be further exacerbated if financially unsophisticated consumers are less likely to shop for low-APR credit cards (Stango and Zinman (2016)).
The second financial outcome that I classify as a financial mistake is an avoidable overdraft fee. An overdraft fee is a fee levied when a withdrawal exceeds the available balance. If the consumer makes a purchase using a debit card linked to an account with insufficient funds, an overdraft fee is levied on the account. In general, overdraft fees are charged on all deposit transactions with insufficient funds. However, in this paper, I focus on overdraft fees arising from debit card purchases; overdraft fees from other types of deposit account transfers are not classified as avoidable overdraft fees.11
How pervasive and costly are avoidable overdraft fees? Panel B of Figure1reports the annual frequency of incurring an avoidable overdraft fee, with the columns sorted by the average number of occurrences. Over the sample period, 71% of consumers have never incurred an avoidable overdraft fee, 15% have incurred on average less than one per year, 6% have incurred on average between one and three per year, 3% have incurred on average between three and six per year, and 4% have incurred on average more than six per year. The cost of incurring an avoidable overdraft fee is just the overdraft fee itself; there is no counterpart of the penalty APR associated with a credit card late fee.
From Panel C, where avoidable late and overdraft fees are considered jointly, we see that 34% of individuals have never incurred an avoidable fee over the five-year sample period, 32% have incurred at least one during the sample period but less than one per year on average, 16% have incurred between one and three per year, 8% have incurred between three and six, and a notable 10% have incurred on average more than six avoidable fees per year, that is, they have incurred at least 30 avoidable fees over the five-year sample period.
In sum, the results indicate that while financial mistakes are pervasive—two-thirds of the sample population have incurred at least one avoidable fee—the majority of the costs are borne by a smaller fraction of the population, around 20% to 25%, who incur multiple avoidable fees per year. This corroborates Low et al. (2017), who find that a small fraction of consumers (less than 10% in their data) account for more than three quarters of all overdraft and nonsufficient funds fees.
How does the frequency of financial mistakes vary across demographic and financial characteristics? TableIreports the demographic and financial characteristics of consumers sorted by the frequency of avoidable fees. Column (1) represents the 34% of consumers who have not incurred avoidable late fees or avoidable overdraft fees during the sample period. The next four columns are sorted into quartiles by annual frequency of avoidable late fees and avoidable overdraft fees. As can be seen, consumers with no avoidable fees are slightly older, but there is no relationship across age conditional on a single mistake.12
The results reveal only a small variation across income levels—the annual income of consumers with zero avoidable fees is just below $70,000, while the average annual income among the quartiles is between $58,000 and $62,000, rising with the frequency of mistakes. In contrast, there is significant variation across both total and liquid asset holdings—total and liquid assets increase from $91,000 and $7,000 to $307,000 and $24,000, respectively, when comparing consumers who have the most avoidable fees with those who have none.
The bank's internal credit score further shows that consumers who never incur avoidable fees have higher credit scores than those who do incur avoidable fees. However, surprisingly, conditional on incurring at least one avoidable fee, consumers who incur many such fees have a higher internal credit score than those who incur few. Debt-to-income ratio and credit card limit utilization both increase with the frequency of mistakes, while the fraction of consumers who hold an investment account with the bank decreases with the frequency of mistakes. The percentage of consumers who have an online or mobile account increases with the frequency of mistakes, as does the median number of monthly logins (conditional on having an account). Finally, customers who incur many avoidable fees live in ZIP codes where proportionally fewer members of the population have a four-year college degree.
In sum, the propensity to incur avoidable fees varies with demographic variables such as age and education, as well as with financial characteristics such as income, credit score, and assets. Importantly, the variation in assets explains a substantial amount of the variation in financial mistakes, which is in line with Scholnick, Massoud, and Saunders (2013) and Choi et al. (2014), who find that poorer individuals are more likely to make mistakes and to make choices that are inconsistent with utility maximization.13
The main premise of the analysis in this paper is that avoidable card fees can be used to measure financial sophistication: consumers who incur many avoidable card fees can be inferred to be financially unsophisticated and therefore less likely to fully understand their financial contracts. In this section, I investigate and discuss the validity of this assumption from three different angles. First, I document that incurring avoidable card fees is a behavior that persists over several years. Second, I show that incurring avoidable card fees is correlated with misallocated credit card debt repayments, misallocated credit card expenditures, and lack of mortgage refinancing. Third, I analyze two alternative mechanisms that could potentially explain the evidence on the incurring of avoidable late fees: liquidity management and optimal inattention. I conclude that neither of these mechanisms is likely to be driving the results.
In this section, I present evidence that incurring avoidable late and overdraft fees is a persistent behavior that can be attributed to individual characteristics, rather than to idiosyncratic shocks alone.
TableIIreports the results of predictive OLS regressions that regress financial mistakes this year avoidable card fees incurred last year. Panel A presents the coefficients from a regression in which the unit of observation is a customer-year, and the dependent variable is an indicator that takes the value one if the customer has made a financial mistake in that year, and zero otherwise. The dependent variable of interest is an indicator for whether the customer made at least one mistake last year (Panel A.i) or more than one mistake last year (Panel A.ii). Additional control variables include age bins, income quintiles, credit score, liquid assets, and total assets (measured last year).
Panel A.i shows that financial mistakes are persistent over time. The probability of incurring an avoidable late fee this year is 15 percentage points higher, or 1.6 times higher, if an avoidable late fee was incurred last year than if it was not. Similarly, having incurred an avoidable overdraft fee last year increases the probability of incurring an avoidable overdraft fee this year by 14 percentage points, or by a factor of two.14
Panel A.ii shows that the persistence in financial mistakes is driven primarily by customers who incur multiple avoidable fees per year. Having incurred more than one avoidable late fee last year increases the probability of incurring one this year by 58 percentage points, or by a factor of four. Similarly, having incurred more than one avoidable overdraft fee last year increases the probability of incurring one this year by 51 percentage points, or by a factor of more than five.15
The difference between Panels A.i and A.ii also highlights a learning effect among part of the population: the probability of making a financial mistake this year actually decreases for consumers who made precisely one mistake last year. However, the effect of learning dissipates entirely for customers who have made multiple mistakes. I explore this effect further in SectionI.Bof theInternet Appendix.
Panel B of TableIIprovides complementary evidence on the persistence of financial mistakes. If financial mistakes are indeed persistent and caused by individual characteristics, then controlling for individual fixed effects should explain a majority of the variation. Panel B tests this hypothesis.
Formally, Panel B compares the performance of a predictive regression with and without customer fixed effects. I run a predictive OLS regression in which the unit of observation is a customer-month and the dependent variable is an indicator for whether a mistake occurred. The control variables include age, income, credit score, liquid assets, and total assets. I use four standard measures of goodness of fit: the adjustedR2$R^2$(which adjusts for the number of predictor variables), the square root of the mean squared error (RMSE), the mean absolute error (MAE), and the correlation between actual and predicted mistakes (the Pearson correlation).
Columns (1) to (3) report the goodness-of-fit measures when regressing only on controls, and columns (4) to (6) report the goodness-of-fit measures when including customer fixed effects. Across all four measures, the goodness of fit increases substantially when we include customer fixed effects. For example, using the adjustedR2$R^2$, we see that only 4% of the variation in avoidable late fees is explained in a linear model by age, income, credit score, and liquid and total assets. When we include customer fixed effects, the adjustedR2$R^2$increases more than sixfold, from 0.04 to 0.27.
A simple variance decomposition corroborates these findings. Using customer-month observations, I decompose the variation in the frequency and cost of financial mistakes into the variation between customers plus the variation within customers:var(yit)=varEyit|i+Evaryit|i$\text{var}(y_{it}) = \text{var}\left(\text{E}\left[y_{it}|i\right]\right)+\text{E}\left[\text{var}\left(y_{it}|i\right)\right]$, wherei$i$andt$t$index customers and months, respectively. I find that the “explained” or “between-customer” variation,varEyit|i$\text{var}\left(\text{E}\left[y_{it}|i\right]\right)$, accounts for 25% (35%) of the variation in the frequency (cost) of avoidable late fees and 24% (32%) of the variation in the frequency (cost) of avoidable overdraft fees.
These results show that financial mistakes are persistent, which suggests that they are caused by individual characteristics rather than random idiosyncratic shocks. In addition, for first-time mistakes there appears to be a short-term reversal consistent with a learning effect, as discussed in Agarwal et al. (2009a). However, this effect dissipates for customers who have made multiple mistakes, with the group of “repeat offenders” who make multiple mistakes year after year (more than a quarter of the sample) bearing the vast majority of the costs.
In a second validity test, I compare the frequency of incurring avoidable late and overdraft fees with other financial choices. The idea is that if avoidable card fees are due at least in part to limited financial sophistication, then consumers who frequently incur avoidable card fees might exhibit similarly suboptimal behavior in other domains. I test this prediction by analyzing three other financial choices that have been noted in the literature as difficult to rationalize using standard models of optimal choice: misallocated credit card debt repayments, misallocated credit card expenditures, and lack of mortgage refinancing.
The first, misallocated credit card debt repayments, relates to how consumers who carry a revolving balance across multiple credit cards allocate their repayments. Such consumers can minimize their interest payments by first paying off the card with the highest effective interest rate. However, Ponce, Seira, and Zamarripa (2017) and Gathergood et al. (2019) show using data from Mexico and the United Kingdom that many cardholders make payments on the “wrong” card (not the highest-interest-rate card). Both papers conclude that their results are more indicative of behavioral and cognitive limitations than rational optimization frictions, which suggests that limited financial sophistication may play a role.16
To analyze credit card debt repayments, I follow the methodology in Gathergood et al. (2019) and categorize credit card debt repayments as a financial mistake when they are not allocated to minimize interest payments. I calculate the amount of credit card repayment misallocation as the difference between the optimal repayment and the observed repayment on the card with the highest APR.17
The second financial choice that I analyze, misallocated credit card expenditures, relates to how consumers with multiple credit cards who carry a revolving balance on at least one card allocate their expenditures. Such consumers can minimize their interest payments by allocating their expenditures to the card with the lowest effective interest rate. For example, consider a consumer with two credit cards, one that has been paid in full (so that all new purchases carry an effective interest rate of 0% until the end of the statement period) and one that has a revolving balance (so that all new purchases immediately incur interest charges at the standard APR or the penalty APR). To minimize interest payments, this consumer should allocate expenditures to the first credit card rather than to the second.
I categorize a credit card expenditure as misallocated (i.e., a financial mistake) if it occurs on a card with a revolving balance while another card with a lower effective interest rate has enough credit available to cover the expenditure. I calculate the amount of credit card expenditure misallocation as the difference between the interest-minimizing expenditures and the observed expenditures on the lowest-APR card (holding fixed the total amount of expenditures).
It is important to note that credit card rewards programs influence optimal spending allocations, depending on customers' idiosyncratic preferences for these features, as discussed in Gathergood et al. (2019). For the purpose of this paper, I disregard rewards programs. However, the existence of rewards programs means that it is more difficult to argue that misallocated credit card expenditures are a strictly dominated choice, as is the case with avoidable late and overdraft fees.
The third financial choice that I analyze is lack of mortgage refinancing. Within the sample of consumers who hold mortgages with my bank, I analyze whether they fail to refinance their mortgages when it would have been optimal to do so. Using a sample of U.S. households in December 2010, Keys, Pope, and Pope (2016) (KPP) estimate that approximately 20% of households for whom refinancing was optimal had not refinanced, and that the discounted present value of the cost of this choice, for the median household, was $11,500. Failure to refinance is, in the words of the authors, “a particularly large consumer financial mistake.” Like avoidable card fees and credit card debt and expenditure misallocations, failure to refinance is consistent with financially unsophisticated behavior.18
I replicate the findings of KPP. Specifically, I restrict the sample to consumers with a mortgage who have a prime credit score and whose current combined loan-to-value (CLTV) ratio is below 90%. I then calculate the present discounted value of refinancing, assuming that the consumer refinances at the current Freddie Mac Primary Mortgage Market Survey (PMMS) rate for a 30-year fixed-rate mortgage (FRM), and apply the optimal refinancing formula of Agarwal, Driscoll, and Laibson (2013b) using the same parameter values as in KPP.19I find that 24% of consumers in the unconstrained sample fail to optimally refinance their mortgage. This is slightly higher than the 20% in KPP, which reflects the fact that the 30-year FRM Freddie Mac PMMS rate was lower in most of my sample period than it was in the period studied by KPP (December 2010).20
TableIIIshows that financial mistakes are correlated across multiple domains. The table reports the average frequency of a financial mistake, tabulated by the frequency of incurring an avoidable late fee (Panel A) and the frequency of incurring an avoidable overdraft fee (Panel B). As can be seen, there is a positive correlation between the two benchmark financial mistakes, as consumers who frequently incur avoidable late fees are more prone to incurring avoidable overdraft fees and vice versa. Moreover, the two benchmark mistakes are positively correlated with all three additional mistakes. For example, among the consumers who have never incurred an avoidable late fee, 54% have misallocated their debt repayments, while among the consumers who have incurred the most avoidable late fees, 66% have misallocated their debt repayments. This positive correlation is present when comparing both debt repayment misallocation and expenditure misallocation with both avoidable late fees and avoidable overdraft fees.
In addition, we can see that mortgage refinancing mistakes are correlated with avoidable card fee mistakes, as consumers who fail to refinance their mortgages are also more likely to incur avoidable card fees. For example, among unconstrained mortgage owners who have never incurred avoidable late (overdraft) fees, only 20% (19%) should optimally refinance, while among the highest quartile by frequency of avoidable late (overdraft) fees, 28% (30%) should optimally refinance. These findings suggest that a lack of financial planning contributes to the variation across consumers in mortgage refinancing, which is consistent with the microlevel evidence in KPP.
In sum, I find that financial mistakes correlate across multiple domains. Consumers who frequently incur avoidable card fees are more likely to misallocate their debt payments and expenditures and less likely to optimally refinance their mortgages, suggesting that the latter more costly decisions are also distorted by optimization failures. Taken together, these results are consistent with the view that financial mistakes are driven largely by a lack of financial sophistication rather than by temporary and idiosyncratic shocks.
What alternative mechanisms could explain the avoidable late and overdraft fees that consumers incur? In a third validity test, I show that consumers are invariant to two potential alternative mechanisms: the economic costs associated with fees, and the transaction costs of avoiding fees. In contrast, higher levels of education are correlated with a lower frequency of avoidable fees.
To examine the role of the economic costs, I calculate the implicit interest rate on late and overdraft fees. This is given by the additional finance charge relative to the minimum payment in the case of late fees, and by the additional finance charge relative to the purchase in the case of overdraft fees. If avoidable late and overdraft fees are driven by rational liquidity demand, then we would expect a negative relationship between the costs and the frequency of the fees. Instead, we find that consumers are invariant to the economic stakes of incurring avoidable fees, as the frequency of financial mistakes does not vary with the implicit cost of the mistake.21
This finding on avoidable late and overdraft fees is hard to reconcile with standard models for time preferences, especially considering that the sample we study includes only consumers who have ample liquidity available. However, this result is similar to the finding in Gathergood et al. (2019) that debt repayment misallocations are invariant to economic stakes.
The second alternative mechanism I explore is whether avoidable late and overdraft fees are driven by time costs. Such costs could arise in the form of transaction costs (if it is time-consuming to pay the minimum balance) and/or as information costs (if it is time-consuming to learn when the minimum payment is due, or to read and understand the true costs associated with missing a payment). Such time costs could lead consumers to be optimally inattentive (Abel, Eberly, and Panageas (2013)).
As a proxy for transaction costs, I use whether consumers have an online or mobile account with the bank, and if so, how often they log in to their accounts. Unconditionally, the evidence contradicts the transaction costs hypothesis: I find that among consumers who have never incurred an avoidable card fee, 88% have an online account and 58% have a mobile account, while among the quartile of consumers who have incurred the most avoidable fees, 92% have an online account and 70% have a mobile account. In addition, the median number of monthly logins is higher for consumers who make many mistakes, with 18 online logins and 12 mobile logins, versus a median of 13 online and six mobile logins for the consumers who never make mistakes.22
TableIVpresents regression coefficients from linear cross-sectional regressions of the annual frequency of avoidable card fees on the proxies for transaction costs. Column (1) reports results for a regression of the annual frequency on a dummy variable that takes the value one if the consumer has an online account. The results show that consumers with an online account incur on average 0.7 more avoidable fees per year, even controlling for income and age. A similar result is seen in column (2), which reports results for a regression of the annual frequency on the average number of online logins. Here, we find that among the customers who hold an online account, those who log in frequently make more financial mistakes on average.
Columns (3), (4), and (5) report results for a regression of the annual frequency on measures of education. The bank data do not include direct observations of customers' education levels. I therefore use average education data from the Census Bureau. For each consumer, I observe the fraction of individuals in their ZIP code with at least a high-school diploma, the fraction with at least a two-year college degree, and the fraction with at least a four-year college degree. Columns (3), (4), and (5) present the regression coefficients on these averages. I find that the regression coefficients for all three measures are negative. A 1% increase in the fraction of households with at least a high-school diploma is related to a 9.6-percentage-point decrease in the probability of a monthly mistake. Note that the unconditional probability of a monthly mistake is 11%. The regression coefficients on the fraction of households with at least two- and four-year college degrees are −8.5% and −8.3%, respectively.
Taken together, these results indicate that incurring avoidable card fees is more likely to be driven by a lack of understanding of financial contracts (which is potentially related to education) than by pure time costs.
article.III. Financial Sophistication and Spendi:
The aim of the empirical analysis is to examine how financial sophistication affects consumers' savings behavior and their spending responses to changes in income. In this section, I analyze whether financially unsophisticated consumers, who may not fully understand their financial contracts, display excessive sensitivity to changes in disposable income, even when those changes would be predictable to the econometrician.
I estimate the effect of financial sophistication on spending responses using a DID event study of consumers with a HELOC. The analysis exploits two specific features that make the HELOC a suitable setting: (i) HELOC customers are wealthy and have high levels of liquidity,23and (ii) a HELOC involves a predetermined increase in monthly payments that allows me to distinguish the effect of sophistication from that of liquidity constraints. Specifically, the payments for a HELOC are structured across two periods, namely, adrawperiod and, following areset date, arepaymentperiod. During the draw period, which typically lasts five or 10 years, the HELOC is an open-ended, nonamortizing line of credit, which means that the consumer pays interest only. In the repayment period, the HELOC converts to a closed-end amortizing loan, and the consumer pays both interest and principal over 10 or 20 years. Importantly for this study, the change in monthly payments is fixed and is known on the date the contract is signed, at least 10 years in advance.
As discussed in Ganong and Noel (2019), it is important to study a predictablenegativechange in disposable income to distinguish behavioral effects from the effects of liquidity constraints. In the face of a positive change in income, the effects of liquidity constraints and a lack of sophistication go in the same direction, complicated by the fact that these two characteristics are positively correlated empirically. By contrast, in the face of a negative change, the spending response of a consumer who lacks understanding is qualitatively different from that of a sophisticated consumer facing liquidity constraints.24
SectionI.Ain theInternet Appendixprovides additional background on HELOC pricing and terms, and FigureIA.1provides examples of how different U.S. financial institutions visually communicate to consumers the HELOC payment paths across the draw period and the repayment period.
In regression (3), the vectorβ$\beta$contains the main coefficients of interest. For each month, it measures the average consumption outcome relative to the first month of the repayment period. In regression (4), I split the sample into five groups based on financial mistakes. To do so, I first measure the monthly frequency of incurring avoidable late or overdraft fees up untilt=0$t=0$. I then assign all customers who have incurred no avoidable fees toGroup0$Group_0$. The remaining customers are split into quartiles based on their frequency of financial mistakes. In this regression, the matrixδ$\delta$contains the main coefficients of interest. For each month, it measures the average change in the consumption outcome, relative to month 0, for an individual who has incurred avoidable fees, over and above the average change over the same period for an individual who has incurred no avoidable fees. Regression (4) thus yields DID estimates of how financial sophistication affects the consumption response to a HELOC reset. All point estimates are reported with standard errors clustered at the state-year level.
For expositional simplicity, I sometimes employ a compact version of (4) in which the consumption outcome is averaged within the periods−6$-6$to−1$-1$(“pre”) and 1 to 6 (“post”). With the data set collapsed to only two time periods, the DID estimates are expressed by the interaction termGroupi×Post$Group_i\times Post$. Whenever I employ the compact model, I report the corresponding results from the full model in TableIA.IV.
The main methodological challenge is that financial sophistication is correlated with liquid assets. As described in SectionsII.CandIV, sophisticated individuals who incur few or no avoidable fees also tend to have high levels of total and liquid assets. For instance, it may be the case that no customers understand their HELOC contracts, in which case all customers, regardless of how many avoidable fees they have previously incurred, are surprised by the increase in their monthly mortgage payments. In this case, the variation in consumption responses is driven entirely by differences in access to liquid assets. While liquidity is less likely to be a constraint in the context of this paper, given the above, I further address this identification issue in two additional ways.
First, the DID estimates are conditional on a comprehensive set of controls that include liquid assets. Following Jensen and Johannesen (2017), for each control variable I include the value att=0$t=0$and interact it with the full vector of months. This procedure identifies the effect via a comparison of individuals who have incurred avoidable fees with individuals who have not, but who have the same observed characteristics. In particular, the empirical design compares individuals with the same amount of liquid assets and the same credit score. The main specification includes the following covariates (all interacted with a full set of month dummies): age (dummy for each two-year bin), income (quintiles), liquid assets (quintiles), total assets (quintiles), and credit score (quintiles).
Second, in a robustness test, I run the DID estimator using a synthetic control group. Specifically, I let the group of consumers with the highest frequency of financial mistakes, those in the fourth quartile (Group4$Group_4$), be the “treatment” group. I match each individual from the treatment group with an individual from the group of consumers who have incurred no avoidable fees, using a matching algorithm that picks the nearest neighbor based on liquid assets, credit score, and HELOC reset amount (without replacement). I then run the DID estimator comparing these two groups. The estimator thus compares the change in spending for financially unsophisticated individuals to a synthetic control group of sophisticated individuals that has, mechanically, the same distribution of liquid assets, credit score, and HELOC reset amount. Panel B of TableVreports the summary statistics for the treatment group, the actual control group (Group0$Group_0$), and the synthetic control group.
A second methodological challenge is that financially sophisticated consumers have drawn less on their HELOC accounts relative to financially unsophisticated consumers. As shown in Panel B of TableV, consumers inGroup0$Group_0$have an average HELOC reset of $337 while consumers inGroup4$Group_4$have an average HELOC reset of $521. Variation in consumption responses may therefore be due to variation in HELOC reset amounts. I address this identification issue in two ways.
First, using the synthetic control group, I isolate the effect of variation in HELOC reset amounts. Recall that one of the three matching variables is the HELOC reset amount. Mechanically, the synthetic control group compares the consumption response of financially unsophisticated and financially sophisticated consumers with exactly the same HELOC reset amounts.
Finally, in an additional robustness test, I run the DID estimator where the dependent variable,yit$y_{it}$, is the consumption expenditure for individuali$i$at timet$t$scaled by the HELOC reset amount for individuali$i$. By scaling the consumption expenditure by the reset amount, this estimator compares changes in consumer spending around the reset date for financially unsophisticated individuals relative to financially sophisticated individualsrelativeto their respective changes in their monthly HELOC payments.
On average, consumers cut their consumption expenditures after their HELOC reset. Figure2, Panel A illustrates this result with a plot of the estimated coefficients on the time dummies (i.e., the elements in the vectorβ$\beta$). The point estimates in the six months leading up to HELOC reset are positive but statistically indistinguishable from zero, implying that on average consumers do not change their consumption expenditures in the six months leading up to their HELOC reset. The point estimates in the six months following HELOC reset are negative, declining, and statistically significant, implying that consumers cut their consumption in the months following their HELOC reset. On average, the monthly consumption expenditure is $57 lower three months after the reset, and $68 lower six months after the reset.
Figure2, Panel B illustrates that financially unsophisticated individuals cut their spending, while financially sophisticated individuals do not. The figure plots the coefficients on the month dummies when the model (3) is run separately forGroup0$Group_0$andGroup4$Group_4$. The solid line plots the coefficients for consumers with no financial mistakes (Group0$Group_0$). While the point estimates are positive before the reset and negative after the reset, all point estimates are statistically insignificant. This implies that financially sophisticated individuals smooth their consumption expenditures around their HELOC reset. The dotted line plots the coefficients for financially unsophisticated individuals, that is, those in the top quartile based on financial mistakes. The point estimates for this sample are positive but insignificant before HELOC reset, and negative and significant after. The monthly consumption expenditure is $184 lower three months after the reset, and $198 lower six months after the reset. TableIA.IVreports point estimates and standard errors for model (3), both for the full sample and for each group, and FigureIA.6plots the coefficient estimates for all five groups.
The DID regressions confirm these results. Panel A of TableVreports the regression coefficients for the compact DID model, and we see that following HELOC reset, financially unsophisticated individuals cut their spending significantly more than financially sophisticated individuals. Individuals in the third and fourth quartiles based on financial mistakes (Groups 3 and 4) cut their monthly consumption expenditures by $70 and $162 more, respectively, than individuals with no financial mistakes (Group 0). Individuals in Groups 1 and 2, those with a few financial mistakes, cut their monthly consumption expenditures by $2 and $24 more, respectively, than individuals in Group 0.
To study how the set of controls shapes the results, I estimate the compact model while moving sequentially from a specification with no controls to a specification with all controls. The most parsimonious specification, with no controls (Panel A, column (1)), implies that the average difference between pre- and postreset consumption expenditures for Group 4, over and above the difference for Group 0, is −$215. The estimate is virtually the same, −$219, when I add fixed effects for every two-year age bin (column (2)). It turns out that part of the variation is driven by differences in income and total assets; when I control for age, income quintiles, and total assets (column (3)), the DID estimate is −$190. Additional variation is driven by differences in liquid assets and credit scores; when I control for age, income, total assets, liquid assets, and credit scores, the DID estimate is −$162.
Column (5) in Panel A reports the DID estimate when comparing Group 4 with the synthetic control group. The synthetic control group is created by matching on credit score, liquid assets, and HELOC reset amount, and the regression also controls for age, income, and total assets. The coefficient estimate implies that the average difference between pre- and postreset consumption expenditures for Group 4, over that of the synthetic control group, is −$126. This result indicates that individuals with a history of financial mistakes reduce their consumption expenditures significantly more after HELOC reset than individuals with the same credit score, liquid assets, and reset amount who have no history of financial mistakes.
Panel B provides summary statistics for the control group (Group 0), the treatment group (Group 4), and the synthetic control group. By construction, Group 4 and the synthetic control group have similar credit scores, liquid assets, and reset amounts, the control group has a marginally higher HELOC limit at the time of reset, the average age and income in the two groups are similar, and average total assets are higher in the control group.
Column (6) in Panel A reports the DID estimate when the outcome variable is scaled by the HELOC reset amount. The results are qualitatively similar to those in the benchmark DID estimator, except for Group 3, where the results are still negative but no longer statistically significant. Individuals in Groups 1 and 2, those with a few financial mistakes, cut their monthly consumption expenditures relative to their HELOC reset amounts by 1 and 3 percentage points more, respectively, than individuals in Group 0. Individuals in the third and fourth quartiles based on financial mistakes (Groups 3 and 4) cut their monthly consumption expenditures relative to their HELOC reset amounts by 11 and 27 percentage points more, respectively, than individuals with no financial mistakes (Group 0). (Only the coefficient on Group 4 is statistically distinguishable from Group 0 at the 1% level.)
To study how the spending responses vary across individual expenditure categories, I run the compact DID model for 16 different expenditure categories. TableVIreports the estimates. Following the Lusardi (1996) taxomony, each expenditure category is classified as durable (Panel A), strict nondurable (Panel B), or other nondurable (Panel C).25The results indicate that the change in expenditures following HELOC reset is not merely part of a financial plan to front-load durable expenditures. Financially unsophisticated individuals do cut their consumption expenditures in durables such as automobiles (−$4) and electronics (−$7), but they also cut their expenditures in nondurables such as fuel (−$7) and groceries (−$37), as well as in necessities such as healthcare copayments (−$10) and drugstore purchases (−$7).
To summarize, after a HELOC reset, financially unsophisticated individuals cut their consumption more than financially sophisticated individuals—in terms of both the absolute change and the relative change. This effect holds even for individuals with similar liquid assets, credit scores, and similar HELOC reset amounts, which is consistent with the idea that, unlike financially sophisticated consumers, financially unsophisticated consumers might not fully understand the information in their financial contracts.

As of July 2016, his payment will rise to more than $1,100 a month from the $400 he is paying to cover just the interest. ‘We both now live on a fixed income and will not be able to make the payments,’ he said of himself and his girlfriend.” (Andrew Khouri and E. In addition, in a 2016 survey, TD Bank found that more than one-quarter of homeowners with HELOCs did not know when their HELOC draw period ended. In total, 812 completed surveys were collected. One question asked what impact respondents anticipated the draw period expiration would have on their monthly payments. Of the respondents, 19% answered (correctly) that their payments would increase, 38% answered (incorrectly) that their payments would decrease, and 43% answered that they did not know how the ending of the draw period would affect their payments (TD Bank (2016)).
