article.IV. Financial Sophistication and Savings:
In this section, I explore anindirecteffect of financial sophistication on spending responses, namely, the effect due to savings rates. The estimates presented above (given byβ$\beta$andδ$\delta$with the full set of controls from (3) and (4), respectively) capture only thedirecteffect of financial sophistication, in the sense that they hold liquid assets fixed. However, liquid assets are endogenously determined by the savings and spending behavior of the consumer, and thus are influenced by the level of financial sophistication through the savings channel. If financially unsophisticated consumers have lower savings rates and fewer financial buffers than financially sophisticated consumers, then the total effect of a lack of sophistication on spending responses will be even larger since having lower liquid savings mechanically exacerbates spending responses.26
To study this indirect effect, I analyze whether financial sophistication affects consumers' financial buffers, that is, their liquid assets, debt-to-income ratios, and credit scores. After presenting differences in savings rates, I document that current measures of financial sophistication predict future measures of financial buffers. Finally, to explore the economic mechanism at play, I present complementary evidence on differences in expenditure rates.
I find that consumers who make financial mistakes also display lower savings rates. Column (1) of TableVIIIreports estimates from regression (6) below, where the dependent variable is the individual savings rate. The coefficients represent the conditional difference for each financial mistake group, relative to the group of individuals who have made no financial mistakes (Group 0).
The savings rates of consumers who make few mistakes—those in Groups 1 and 2—are on average 1 and 2 percentage points lower, respectively, although these coefficient estimates are not statistically significant. Consumers who make more mistakes, those in Groups 3 and 4, have savings rates that are on average 6 and 7 percentage points lower, respectively. As savings rates vary based on age, income, and wealth (Dynan, Skinner, and Zeldes (2004), Fagereng et al. (2019)), the regressions control for age, income, total assets, liquid assets, and credit score (measured as a monthly average over the same time period).27
These estimates represent differences innetsavings rates, in the sense that they are net of capital gains. If the rate of return on savings is positively correlated with financial sophistication, then the differences in thegrosssavings rate will be even higher. For example, van Rooij, Lusardi, and Alessie (2011b) find that individuals who display low financial knowledge are less likely to invest in stocks. I find additional supportive evidence for this result in my sample. Specifically, I find that financially unsophisticated consumers are less likely to hold a brokerage account. See SectionI.Dof theInternet Appendixfor details.
Next, I study whether lower savings rates lead to fewer financial buffers. If financially unsophisticated consumers hold fewer financial buffers and have less financial “slack,” then this will mechanically exacerbate their spending responses to income shocks. TableIreports the unconditional means for three measures of financial buffers: liquid assets, credit scores, and debt-to-income ratios. As discussed in SectionII.C, we see that financially unsophisticated consumers are worse off along all three measures. They hold fewer liquid assets and have both lower credit scores and higher debt-to-income ratios.
In (6), the dependent variable,yi$y_i$, is the natural logarithm of liquid assets, the debt-to-income ratio, or the credit score for individuali. The variableXi$X_i$is a vector of control variables, including age bins, income, total and liquid assets, and credit score. As before, the sample is split into five groups,Group0$Group_0$toGroup4$Group_4$, based on the frequency of financial mistakes across the full-sample period. Consumers with no financial mistakes are assigned toGroup0$Group_0$, and the remaining consumers are split into quartiles based on their frequency of financial mistakes. The vectorβ$\beta$contains the coefficients of interest, capturing the difference in financial buffers between consumers who have made financial mistakes and consumers who have not, conditional on other observable variables.
In (7), each variable is measured over a two-year period instead of over the full-sample period. The dependent variable,yi,2015−2016$y_{i,2015-2016}$, is measured over 2015 and 2016, and the independent variables,Groupj,2013−2014$Group_{j,2013-2014}$andXi,2013−2014$X_{i,2013-2014}$, are measured over 2013 and 2014. The vectorδ$\delta$contains the coefficients of interest. It captures how a current measure of financial sophistication predicts future values of financial buffers.
TableVIIpresents the regression coefficients from (6) and (7). The coefficient estimates in columns (1) and (2) imply that financially unsophisticated consumers have lower levels of liquid assets and higher debt-to-income ratios than financially sophisticated consumers. For Groups 1 to 4, the point estimates relative to Group 0 are negative (positive) for liquid assets (debt-to-income ratio), although the difference in liquid assets between Groups 1 and 0 is not statistically significant. It is worth noting that differences in liquid assets and debt-to-income ratios increase with the intensity of financial mistakes: consumers who are categorized as most financially unsophisticated (those in Group 4) hold 63% fewer liquid assets and have 12% higher debt-to-income ratios than financially sophisticated consumers. In column (3), we see that financially unsophisticated consumers also have lower credit scores. However, these estimates do not increase with the intensity of financial mistakes. Rather, consumers in Group 1 display the largest conditional difference in credit scores, −11%, relative to Group 0, while the point estimate for Group 4 is small, −3.6%, and not statistically significant.28
Columns (4), (5), and (6) of TableVIIshow that financial sophistication predicts future differences in financial buffers: consumers who made financial mistakes in 2013 and 2014 have lower liquid assets and higher debt-to-income ratios in 2015 and 2016, while there is no statistically significant difference in credit scores. As before, the estimates for liquid assets and debt-to-income ratios increase with the intensity of financial mistakes. The consumers who are most financially unsophisticated, those in Group 4 as measured in 2013 and 2014, hold 50% fewer liquid assets and have 10% higher debt-to-income ratios in 2015 and 2016 than the most financially sophisticated consumers, those in Group 0.
To shed light on the economic mechanism driving lower savings rates (and, mechanically, higher expenditure rates), I next examine what types of goods financially unsophisticated individuals spend their money on. To do so, I calculate expenditure rates across three categories: necessity goods, luxury goods, and other goods. Necessity goods are defined as goods for which the share of total spending on the good declines as income increases, luxury goods are goods for which this share increases, and other goods are goods for which there is no significant increase or decrease. I regress individual expenditure rates for these three categories on dummies for each financial mistake group (where Group 0 is the omitted group) and the usual set of controls.
Columns (2) to (4) of TableVIIIreport the regression coefficients. Necessity goods are shown in column (2), luxury goods in column (3), and other goods in column (4). (FigureIA.8shows a binned scatterplot of Engel curves for each expenditure category.) For all three categories, the expenditure rates are positive and increase as we move from Group 1 to Group 4. That is, across all categories, financially unsophisticated individuals spend more money than do financially sophisticated individuals, although the only statistically significant coefficients are those for luxury goods in Groups 2 to 4 and other goods in Groups 3 and 4. The coefficients are largest (and most statistically significant) for luxury goods. For example, individuals who make many financial mistakes, those in Groups 3 and 4, spend respectively 2.6% and 3.2% more of their income on luxury goods than do individuals who never make financial mistakes.
Financially unsophisticated individuals spend more on luxury goods not just relative to their income, but also relative to total expenditures. Figure3shows the fraction of total expenditures spent on luxury goods (entertainment, electronics, jewelry and arts, retail, and travel) for Groups 0 and 4, in a binned scatterplot of 30 equal-sized bins based on annual income. The plot also includes a locally linear regression run on the entire data set. Across the income distribution, individuals in Group 4 spend a higher share of total expenditures on luxury goods than those in Group 0.29
article.V. Discussion:
In this section, I discuss the interpretation and potential implications of the empirical results. First, I evaluate the extent to which the empirical results can help us distinguish between classes of theoretical models of financial sophistication. Second, I compare the measures of financial sophistication in this paper—avoidable late fees and overdraft fees—with other measures proposed in the literature, and I argue that their differences highlight a trade-off between efficacy and statistical power.
To what extent can the empirical results help us distinguish between models of financial sophistication and consumer spending? As mentioned in the literature review, the literature provides two distinct classes of models, broadly speaking. In the classical models of consumer spending, consumers have rational attention and are perfectly financially sophisticated. In this class of models, any variation in liquid assets and consumer spending responses is driven by idiosyncratic income shocks (Kaplan and Violante (2014), Kaplan, Moll, and Violante (2018)). The empirical results in this paper are inconsistent with the classical models of rational-attention, financially sophisticated consumers. Intuitively, the prediction from this class of models is inconsistent with a significant spending response to HELOC reset.30
In contrast, a younger class of behavioral models do not assume that consumers are entirely rational, but rather allow them to differ in their degree of financial sophistication. Within this class of models, there exist several different approaches to modeling financial sophistication. While several of these subclasses of behavioral models are consistent with the empirical results in this paper, their different approaches to modeling financial sophistication provide distinct economic interpretations and implications for optimal policy.
For example, a number of seminal papers study the extent to which consumers are either “aware” or “naive” with respect to their behavioral biases (Laibson (1997), O'Donoghue and Rabin (1999)). This literature typically studies the distinction between aware and naive consumers in the context of present bias, but the distinction is also relevant in the context of financial sophistication. For example, under a more aware form, an unsophisticated individual might recognize that she is likely to take on too much debt in the future. In this case, she may choose not to smooth her negative income shock as the extra current debt acts as a commitment device (Laibson (1997)). On the other hand, under a more naive form, an unsophisticated consumer does not realize that the income change was foreseeable in the first place, and she simply consumes hand-to-mouth in lockstep with her available liquidity.
Another example of a class of behavioral models is given by a recent set of papers that theorize that consumers face a type of “information friction” that leaves them without perfect knowledge of their financial contracts or assets (Gabaix (2014), Lian (2023)). Through the lens of “information frictions,” one can interpret the empirical results to indicate that individuals who incur many financial mistakes have a higher cost of acquiring (and potentially retaining) information from their HELOC contracts. These unsophisticated consumers are surprised by the HELOC reset, and they cut their expenditures in response to what appears to them to be an unpredictable income shock. In contrast, financially sophisticated consumers have a low cost of acquiring information from their contracts, and hence they smooth their expenditures around what appears to them to be a predictable income change.31
While several of these subclasses of behavioral models can generate predictions in line with the empirical findings in this paper, the exact economic mechanism is of vital interest to policymakers. For example, the distribution of information costs across contracts and households will likely affect the effectiveness of nudges and similar policy interventions. For example, if information costs are mostly randomly distributed or vary substantially over time, nudges could have a significant effect (e.g., if some consumers randomly forget to open their mail and fail to read their HELOC contracts), while if information costs are highly concentrated and invariant over time, policy nudges will be much less effective (e.g., if some consumers are financially illiterate and incapable of understanding their financial contracts regardless of well-meaning nudges).
Distinguishing between these types of behavioral models is outside the scope of this paper and is left for future research.
Researchers measuring financial sophistication often face a trade-off between efficacy on the one hand and feasibility and statistical power on the other. One method commonly employed in the literature is directly surveying households using a survey designed to elicit financial literacy. For example, van Rooij, Lusardi, and Alessie (2011b) and Agarwal et al. (2015a) employ a survey of financial literacy in The Netherlands and India, respectively. While this method offers a high degree of efficacy, the feasibility of the research study relies on access to household survey data (or on data that can be merged with survey responses).
The findings in SectionIIsuggest that in lieu of using direct psychometric measures of financial sophistication based on expensive surveys of millions of consumers, policymakers may benefit from using observed financial mistakes to measure variation in financial sophistication. However, it is not always obvious which and how many financial mistakes to include in a measure of financial sophistication, as the literature has identified numerous consumer choices that are hard to rationalize using models of optimal choice (Agarwal, Chomsisengphet, and Lim (2017b)).
The choice of which and how many financial mistakes to include in the measure of financial sophistication depends on two factors. The first factor is how easily one can demonstrate that the financial choice is indeed a mistake. For example, the financial choices studied in the literature include both simple situations with unambiguously optimal choices, such as avoiding overdraft fees when possible, and more complex situations in which the optimal choice is potentially sensitive to individual consumer circumstances, such as mortgage refinancing. The second factor is how large a sample of the population can be classified based on this mistake. For example, if the measure of financial sophistication includes appropriate mortgage refinancing, the sample is restricted to homeowners with sufficient equity and credit available.
In this paper, the choice of avoidable late fees and overdraft fees is an attempt to balance this trade-off. Avoidable fees are not the most serious or costly mistakes; they are, however, unambiguous mistakes. Campbell (2016) makes the analogy of charging Mafia bosses with tax evasion, which is not their most serious crime but is the easiest to prove. In addition, while most consumers have a credit or debit card and thus are potentially susceptible to incurring late and overdraft fees, the liquidity threshold slightly biases the sample toward wealthier consumers.
article.VI. Conclusion:
This paper explores how financial sophistication affects consumers' spending responses to changes in income. The analysis shows that financially unsophisticated consumers, unlike financially sophisticated consumers, display significant spending responses to predictable decreases in mortgage payments, a phenomenon not accounted for by standard models that assume full information and attribute spending responses solely to liquidity constraints. In addition, financially unsophisticated consumers have lower savings rates, lower liquid assets, and higher debt-to-income ratios, which leaves them more exposed to unpredictable income shocks. Taken together, these results highlight the importance of financial sophistication as a factor in modeling consumers' savings rates and spending responses.
The findings point to several interesting areas for future research and potential implications for public policy. First, they can provide empirical underpinnings for theoretical models of the effects of financial sophistication on consumer spending. For example, Lian (2023) develops a theoretical model describing consumers who misperceive their wealth and display excessive consumption responses to changes in income.
Second, transaction-level data on financial decisions can be used to study the effects of financial sophistication in other domains, such as entrepreneurship and economic policy. For example, Weber (2018) replicates the measure of financial sophistication from this paper and finds that financially sophisticated individuals are more successful at starting and managing businesses, while D'Acunto et al. (2019) find that in financial choices such as the use of leverage, individuals with low IQ show less response to changes in monetary and fiscal policy.
Finally, the financial mistakes studied in this paper are fairly simple, and thus there is scope for them to be prevented by policies and products that “nudge” consumers (Thaler and Sunstein (2008)). Examples of this are given in two recent papers that study late fees and overdraft fees using data from the United Kingdom. Gathergood et al. (2021) find that autopayment reduces late fees, and Caflisch et al. (2018) find that overdraft alerts via SMS reduce overdraft charges. However, as shown in Medina (2021), well-intentioned nudges (e.g., SMS alerts) may have poor side effects if they cause consumers to overdraw from other accounts. Furthermore, in the present paper I find that a lack of financial sophistication has effects across multiple domains where easy fixes can prove insufficient. This leads to an interesting set of constraints for policymakers designing financial regulations: they need to trade off the benefits of regulation for financially unsophisticated consumers—taking into account that only some aspects of such consumers' behavior might be impacted—with the costs of regulation for other financial market participants.
